does: This paper proposes Value-Anchored Group Policy Optimization (VGPO), a framework that extends Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for alignment of flow-matching-based image generation models. The core contribution addresses two fundamental limitations in applying GRPO to visual synthesis: temporal credit assignment and training stagnation from reward signal depletion. VGPO transforms sparse terminal rewards into dense, temporally-aware value estimates that reflect the criticality of different generation phases, from early structure formation through late refinement. The method anchors value estimation across both temporal dimensions (accounting for timestep-specific importance in the diffusion process) and group dimensions (maintaining optimization signal diversity across training). The framework enables more precise credit assignment for intermediate generation steps while preventing convergence-induced optimization collapse through better reward structure preservation.
connects: This work builds on the recent success of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) in aligning large language models, which itself represents an advance in reinforcement learning-based alignment combining group-relative reward signals with policy optimization. The paper identifies a fundamental gap between GRPO's design assumptions, developed for sequential token generation in LLMs, and the distinct dynamics of iterative refinement in diffusion-based image synthesis. It connects to broader literature on policy optimization for diffusion models, credit assignment problems in RL with long horizons, and the challenge of maintaining optimization signals during convergence. The work responds to recent efforts adapting language model alignment techniques to visual generation, recognizing that naive application of terminal-reward-based methods fails to capture the multiscale temporal structure inherent in diffusion processes where early and late denoising steps play categorically different roles.
enables: This framework enables more effective fine-tuning of flow-matching and diffusion-based generative models for alignment objectives including human preference matching, safety criteria, and task-specific constraints. By solving the temporal credit assignment problem, VGPO could facilitate better optimization of intermediate generation behavior, potentially improving both aesthetic quality and controllability of image synthesis. The approach of anchoring values across multiple dimensions—temporal and group-based—points toward broader methodological advances in applying RL alignment techniques to iterative generative processes beyond images, including video generation and autoregressive audio synthesis. The prevention of optimization stagnation through reward diversity preservation suggests avenues for more stable long-horizon training of complex generative models. Future work could explore how similar dimensional decomposition of value estimation applies to other alignment problems where sparse rewards and phase-dependent criticality create optimization challenges.
